perturbation.original_text: The parties agree that any breach of either of the parties' obligations regarding trademarks, service marks or trade names, confidentiality and/or User Data would result in irreparable injury for which there is no adequate remedy at law. Therefore, in the event of any breach or threatened breach of a party's obligations regarding trademarks, service marks or trade names or confidentiality, the aggrieved party will be entitled to seek equitable relief in addition to its other available legal remedies in a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties acknowledge that a breach of either party's obligations regarding trademarks, service marks, or trade names, confidentiality, and/or User Data *might* result in injury. Therefore, in the event of *a possible* breach or threatened breach of a party's obligations regarding trademarks, service marks or trade names or confidentiality, the aggrieved party *may* be entitled to seek equitable relief in addition to its other available legal remedies in a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces uncertainty by replacing definitive statements with weaker, less certain language. Using 'might' and 'may' instead of 'would' and 'will' significantly weakens the enforceability of the clause, making it ambiguous whether equitable relief is actually warranted or available.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 14402 addresses trademark infringement and unfair competition, and allows for injunctive relief (a form of equitable relief) when there is a likelihood of injury to business reputation or dilution of the distinctive quality of a mark. By making the entitlement to equitable relief uncertain ('may be entitled') and suggesting that injury 'might' result, the modified text contradicts the intent of CA Bus. & Prof. Code § 14402, which aims to provide a clear legal pathway for trademark owners to protect their rights and seek injunctive relief when their trademarks are threatened. This ambiguity undermines the protection afforded by California law to trademark owners.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 14402 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 6. BUSINESS RIGHTS [14000 - 14704] ( Division 6 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 56. ) CHAPTER 3. Trade Names and Designations [14401 - 14495] ( Chapter 3 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 59. ) ARTICLE 1. Trade Names [14401 - 14405] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 59. ) 14402. Any court of competent jurisdiction may restrain, by injunction, any use of trade names in violation of the rights defined in this chapter. (Added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 59.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the entitlement to equitable relief for trademark infringement, weakening the protection offered by California law. By replacing 'will' with 'may' and 'would' with 'might,' the modified text contradicts the intent of California Business and Professions Code § 14402. The original text ensures a stronger legal pathway for trademark owners to seek injunctive relief when their trademarks are threatened, whereas the changed text undermines this by introducing uncertainty, thus creating an ambiguous legal obligation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, BPC 14402, states that any court 'may restrain, by injunction, any use of trade names in violation of the rights defined in this chapter'. While the law itself uses 'may', the original contract strengthens this, while the modified contract significantly weakens the original intent, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Client will maintain accurate records with respect to the calculation of all payments due under this Agreement. Once per year, the parties will review these records to verify the accuracy and appropriate accounting of all payments made pursuant to the Agreement. In addition, Excite may, upon no less than thirty (30) days prior written notice to Client, cause an independent Certified Public Accountant to inspect the records of Client reasonably related to the calculation of such payments during Client's normal business hours.
perturbation.changed_text: Client will keep records related to the calculation of payments. At some point, the parties might look over these records. Excite could potentially have an accountant take a look at Client's records if they give them some notice.
perturbation.explanation: The original text creates a clear obligation for accurate record-keeping and allows for an audit. The modified text introduces ambiguity by using vague language like 'keep records related,' 'at some point,' 'might look over,' and 'could potentially have an accountant take a look.' This makes the obligation for accurate record-keeping and the right to audit unclear and discretionary.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 2031.010 allows for the inspection of documents during discovery in a lawsuit. The modified text, by weakening the obligations to maintain accurate records and the right of inspection, would impede the ability to obtain accurate information during discovery. The deliberate vagueness in the modified text contradicts the spirit and purpose of this law, making it more difficult to enforce accurate accounting and payment obligations.

MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 4. CIVIL DISCOVERY ACT [2016.010 - 2036.050] ( Title 4 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) CHAPTER 14. Inspection, Copying, Testing, Sampling, and Production of Documents, Electronically Stored Information, Tangible Things, Land, and Other Property [2031.010 - 2031.510] ( Heading of Chapter 14 amended by Stats. 2012, Ch. 72, Sec. 30. ) ARTICLE 1. Inspection Demand [2031.010 - 2031.060] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) 2031.010. (a) Any party may obtain discovery within the scope delimited by Chapter 2 (commencing with Section 2017.010), and subject to the restrictions set forth in Chapter 5 (commencing with Section 2019.010), by inspecting, copying, testing, or sampling documents, tangible things, land or other property, and electronically stored information in the possession, custody, or control of any other party to the action. (b) A party may demand that any other party produce and permit the party making the demand, or someone acting on the demanding party’s behalf, to inspect and to copy a document that is in the possession, custody, or control of the party on whom the demand is made. (c) A party ma
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity which undermines the legal obligation related to record keeping and inspection, potentially hindering discovery as described in CCP 2031.010. The original text mandates accurate record-keeping and grants Excite the right to inspect records with prior notice, whereas the changed text weakens these obligations to 'keep records related,' 'might look over,' and 'could potentially have an accountant take a look,' making compliance uncertain. CCP 2031.010(a) states that 'Any party may obtain discovery within the scope delimited by Chapter 2...by inspecting, copying, testing, or sampling documents...in the possession, custody, or control of any other party to the action.' The new language introduces ambiguity, which could impede the discovery process envisioned by CCP 2031.010 by making access to accurate records less certain.

perturbation.original_text: All such message campaigns will comply with Excite's then current privacy policy which is located at http://www.excite.com/privacy_policy and is subject to change from time to time. If the privacy policy changes in a manner that has a material adverse effect on the value, functionality or implementation of the DeliverE message campaign for Client, Excite will notify Client, which will then have the option to cancel future DeliverE campaigns and both parties will be relieved of their obligations related to those canceled DeliverE campaigns, if Client, in its sole but reasonable discretion, finds such changed privacy policy objectionable.
perturbation.changed_text: Such message campaigns will generally adhere to Excite's privacy policy, which may change. If the privacy policy shifts and *potentially* impacts the DeliverE campaign, Excite *might* inform Client, who *could* then opt to end future campaigns if they find the policy *somewhat* objectionable.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing mandatory 'will comply' with 'generally adhere', 'has a material adverse effect' with 'potentially impacts', 'will notify' with 'might inform', 'will then have the option to cancel' with 'could then opt to end', and 'in its sole but reasonable discretion, finds such changed privacy policy objectionable' with 'if they find the policy somewhat objectionable', the modification significantly weakens the protection afforded to the Client concerning privacy policy changes. It introduces ambiguity about the level of compliance required, the obligation to inform, and the conditions under which the Client can cancel future campaigns.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Online Privacy Protection Act (CalOPPA) Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code §§ 22575-22579
perturbation.law_explanation: CalOPPA requires websites that collect personally identifiable information from California residents to conspicuously post a privacy policy. The modified text introduces uncertainty regarding the level of adherence to the privacy policy and the notification process if the policy changes, potentially conflicting with CalOPPA's aim to ensure transparency and inform users about how their data is handled. By weakening the obligation to comply with the policy and to notify the client of changes that have an adverse effect, the text creates a situation where Excite may not fully meet the requirements of CalOPPA, especially concerning clear and conspicuous disclosure of privacy practices and changes to those practices.

SPECIAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [18400 - 22949.92.2] ( Division 8 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 44. ) CHAPTER 22. Internet Privacy Requirements [22575 - 22579] ( Chapter 22 added by Stats. 2003, Ch. 829, Sec. 3. ) 22575. (a) An operator of a commercial Web site or online service that collects personally identifiable information through the Internet about individual consumers residing in California who use or visit its commercial Web site or online service shall conspicuously post its privacy policy on its Web site, or in the case of an operator of an online service, make that policy available in accordance with paragraph (5) of subdivision (b) of Section 22577. An operator shall be in violation of this subdivision only if the operator fails to post its policy within 30 days after being notified of noncompliance. (b) The privacy policy required by subdivision (a) shall do all of the following: (1) Identify the categories of personally identifiable information that the operator collects through the Web site or online service about individual consumers who use or visit its commercial Web site or online service and the categories of third-party persons or entities with whom the operator may share that personally identifiable information. (2)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities regarding compliance with the privacy policy, weakening the obligations outlined in the original text. This contradicts CalOPPA's aim for transparency, as the original text's firm requirements for adherence and notification are replaced with uncertain language. Specifically, the original text states "will comply," ensuring adherence to Excite's privacy policy, while the modified text uses "generally adhere," creating ambiguity. The original text requires Excite to notify Client of material adverse effect changes, whereas the modified version uses "might inform," making notification discretionary. This weakening of the obligation to comply and notify conflicts with the principle that operators of commercial websites shall conspicuously post its privacy policy on its Web site, or in the case of an operator of an online service, make that policy available (BPC 22575(a)).
